nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and standing waters including main rivers, floodplains, and lakes; demersal in sluggish lowland rivers

decorPreferences: dark substrate to reduce stress and enhance coloration
dense planting in background and corners
driftwood and smooth rocks as cover

diet.notes: Predatory; wild diet includes insects, crustaceans, and small fishes. In captivity, offer live foods (small earthworms, bloodworm) and frozen foods (mysis, brine shrimp, frozen prawns, bloodworm). Dried foods are accepted but should not form the bulk of the diet. Feed small, frequent portions to avoid overeating and water fouling.

goodWith: medium-sized barbs (e.g., tiger barbs, tinfoil barbs)
avoidWith: shrimp and small invertebrates (will be consumed)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Parambassis wolffii; confirm availability before using.

summary: One of the largest Ambassidae at up to 20 cm, the Thai Chandid has a translucent body and develops a pronounced nuchal hump at maturity. A predatory shoaling fish from SE Asian lowland rivers, it is often confused with the much smaller Indian Glassy Perch (Parambassis ranga).
careNotes:
The Thai Chandid (Parambassis wolffii) is among the largest members of the Asiatic glassfish family Ambassidae, reaching up to 20 cm in the wild though aquarium specimens are typically somewhat smaller. Like other Parambassis species, the body is largely translucent, revealing the internal skeleton and organs; adult males develop a pronounced nuchal (forehead) hump that distinguishes them from females and from related species.

This species is frequently confused in the trade with the Indian Glassy Perch (Parambassis ranga), which is considerably smaller (to ~8 cm) and has different water parameter preferences. Hobbyists should verify species identity before purchase. Note also that P. ranga is the species most commonly subjected to the cruel practice of fluorescent dye injection (“painted glassfish”), in which fish are injected with artificial dyes to produce unnatural coloration. This practice causes significant suffering and premature death and is strongly opposed by responsible aquarists and trade ethics bodies; avoid purchasing any fish showing unnaturally vivid fluorescent patches.

Despite being sold under the name “Humphead Glassfish,” P. wolffii should not be confused with Parambassis pulcinella, which shares that common name and bears a more dramatically pronounced hump.

P. wolffii is a true freshwater species and does not require salt addition; adding salt is inappropriate for long-term care. Maintain pristine water quality with low nitrates and regular partial water changes. A large, well-filtered aquarium of at least 300 L is recommended for a group of six, with a minimum footprint of 120 cm. Use a dark substrate and dense planting to reduce stress and encourage natural behavior. The species is predatory and will consume any fish or invertebrate small enough to swallow; choose tankmates accordingly. Keep in groups of at least 6 to promote natural shoaling behavior and reduce individual stress.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been documented. In the wild the species is assumed to be an egg scatterer, consistent with other Ambassidae. Large water changes and morning light may trigger spawning attempts in well-conditioned fish, but successful reproduction and fry-rearing in home aquaria have not been reported as of this writing.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Parambassis wolffii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided tank dimensions (48″ × 12″ × 12″, 110 L minimum), water parameters (18–25°C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 8–20°H), native range (Cambodia, Thailand, Laos, Vietnam, Indonesia), diet (live/frozen invertebrates preferred over dried), compatibility (predatory; groups of 6+), and breeding note (not bred in captivity). 300 L used as minimum for a proper group of 6 adults at 20 cm, expanding on the single-specimen 110 L figure.

additional.name: FishBase — Parambassis wolffii
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (20.0 cm SL), temperature range (18–25°C), native range (Chao Phraya, Mekong, Sumatra, Borneo), habitat (sluggish rivers and floodplains, demersal), diet ecology (trophic level 3.7; insects, crustaceans, small fish), family Ambassidae. IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Parambassis wolffii, Thai chandid
additional.notes: Confirmed 20 cm maximum size, shoaling requirement (min 6), predatory nature, tank size (120 cm minimum), diet (live/frozen preferred), and freshwater-only status. Noted dark substrate and dense planting recommendation.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Parambassis wolffii
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Ambassidae), native range (Thailand, Indonesia), etymology (species named for military surgeon Wolff, Bleeker 1850), and IUCN Least Concern (2012).

additional.name: FishBase — Parambassis wolffii (legacy URL)
additional.notes: Alternate FishBase URL (legacy Chanda wolfii synonym). Confirmed discontinuous SE Asian distribution including Chao Phraya and Mekong basins and insular populations on Sumatra and Borneo.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented successful captive breeding. Assumed egg scatterer consistent with Ambassidae family behavior; large water changes and morning light may trigger spawning in conditioned fish but fry-rearing has not been achieved in home aquaria.
